---
title: "Exercise: vector add — your first wgpu kernel"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Compute `c[i] = a[i] + b[i]` on the GPU for a 1024-element pair of `f32` vectors. This is the "hello world" of GPU compute — the entire job is to introduce the **six-step wgpu pipeline** end to end. Every later exercise reuses this scaffolding.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have used [`wgpu::Instance`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/), [`Adapter`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/latest/wgpu/struct.Adapter.html), [`Device`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/latest/wgpu/struct.Device.html), [`Buffer`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/latest/wgpu/struct.Buffer.html), [`ShaderModule`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/latest/wgpu/struct.ShaderModule.html), [`ComputePipeline`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/latest/wgpu/struct.ComputePipeline.html), [`BindGroup`](https://docs.rs/wgpu/latest/wgpu/struct.BindGroup.html), and the `map_async` + `device.poll()` read-back dance.

## Background

The shader is trivially short:

```wgsl
@group(0) @binding(0) var<storage, read>       a: array<f32>;
@group(0) @binding(1) var<storage, read>       b: array<f32>;
@group(0) @binding(2) var<storage, read_write> c: array<f32>;

@compute @workgroup_size(64)
fn main(@builtin(global_invocation_id) gid: vec3<u32>) {
    let i = gid.x;
    if (i >= arrayLength(&c)) { return; }
    c[i] = a[i] + b[i];
}
```

Every later shader in this day is variations on this shape — different bindings, different work in the body, the rest is identical.

The Rust side has to:

1. Acquire an adapter, device, and queue.
2. Upload `a` and `b` into storage buffers; allocate `c` as a storage buffer.
3. Compile the shader into a pipeline; create the bind group.
4. Dispatch enough workgroups to cover N elements.
5. Copy `c` into a host-mappable staging buffer.
6. `map_async` + `device.poll(Wait)` + read.

## The exercise

Open the starter project:

```bash
cd day12/ex-vector-add
```

You will see:

- `src/main.rs` — the wgpu boilerplate is fully written; **one TODO**: fill in the `dispatch_workgroups(...)` call so the workgroup count covers `n` elements.
- `src/shader.wgsl` — provided, complete (the shader above).
- A `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block with three unit tests: one for length, one for values, one for the empty-input edge case.

Your job: write the correct ceiling-division for the workgroup count.

You may **not** change anything else in `main.rs` or `shader.wgsl`.

## How to run

```bash
cd day12/ex-vector-add
cargo test                # three tests
cargo run --release       # runs once and prints the first 8 results
```

When the dispatch math is right, `cargo test` will print:

```
test result: ok. 3 passed; 0 failed
```

The first build downloads `wgpu` and its backend — takes several minutes the first time, seconds afterwards.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — ceiling division"}
You have `n` elements and the shader is declared `@workgroup_size(64)`. Each workgroup processes 64 elements; you need enough workgroups so that `workgroups * 64 ≥ n`.

The standard integer-only ceiling division:

```rust
let workgroups = ((n + 63) / 64) as u32;
```

Equivalent and slightly less error-prone:

```rust
let workgroups = n.div_ceil(64) as u32;     // requires Rust 1.73+
```

If `n == 0`, `workgroups` is 0 — `dispatch_workgroups(0, 1, 1)` is allowed and is a no-op.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — bounds check matters"}
With workgroup size 64 and N = 1000, the last workgroup launches with 64 threads but only 8 of them have data. Without the `if (i >= arrayLength(&c)) { return; }` bounds check in the shader, the extra 56 threads would read and write past the end of the buffer — undefined behaviour.

This is why every WGSL compute shader you write today starts with a bounds check.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
let workgroups = n.div_ceil(64) as u32;
pass.dispatch_workgroups(workgroups, 1, 1);
```

The full reference is in `day12/ex-vector-add-solution/src/main.rs`. The shader and boilerplate are identical to the starter.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Exercise 2** adds a *uniform* buffer for a scalar — same boilerplate, one more binding.
- **Exercise 3** revisits **GC content** (day 1 / day 5) — same shape, but the shader counts bytes via an atomic.
- The boilerplate you just walked through is *exactly* what Burn does internally for every tensor op on the `Wgpu` backend. The high-level `x + y` you wrote on day 11 compiled to a kernel like this one.
